planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall to allow natural cold-moist stratification through winter and spring germination; alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 4–6 weeks at 38–40°F and sow indoors in late winter. Do not grow near food gardens and handle plants only with gloves — all parts are intensely poisonous.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in cool-summer climates, where the deep purple-blue, helmet-shaped flowers on stately 3–5 foot spikes bloom in mid to late summer and provide strong vertical structure to the cottage or woodland border. WARNING: All parts of Aconitum napellus are extremely poisonous — among the most toxic plants in temperate horticulture — containing the alkaloid aconitine, which is lethal in very small amounts through skin absorption or ingestion; always wear gloves when handling and do not plant near edible gardens or where children or pets may access the plant. Cold stratification is required for germination from seed; fall sowing outdoors is the most reliable method. Divide established clumps every 3–4 years in early fall, replanting the tuberous roots at the same depth, and site where cool nights and moderate shade help prevent summer stress.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for best germination results, as delphinium seed loses viability rapidly and cold stratification through winter triggers more uniform spring germination; alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 2–3 weeks at 40°F and sow indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost in cool (55–60°F) conditions.

directions: Plant in rich, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with shelter from strong winds; Delphinium elatum is the species parent of the classic Pacific Giant and Elatum Group hybrid delphiniums — tall, stately cottage garden perennials producing dense, candle-like spikes 3–6 feet tall packed with blue, violet, lavender, or white flowers in late spring to early summer, after which plants can be cut back to the basal foliage to encourage a second flush of bloom in late summer. Delphiniums demand cool summers to perform at their best and decline in zones with prolonged heat and humidity; they are outstanding perennials for the Pacific Northwest, Upper Midwest, and New England, but struggle in the South and southwestern United States. Stake tall stems before they reach full height and protect from slugs in early spring when the new shoots are emerging; divide clumps every 3 years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall to allow natural cold-moist stratification and spring germination; alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 3–4 weeks at 40°F and sow in early spring. Golden columbine self-sows readily and a well-placed colony will naturalize and self-perpetuate over time.

directions: Plant in average to moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Aquilegia chrysantha is a native columbine of the American Southwest — native to canyon walls, stream banks, and moist woodland edges in New Mexico, Arizona, and northern Mexico — and is one of the most heat-tolerant and long-blooming of all columbines, producing exceptionally long-spurred, bright yellow flowers on 3–4 foot stems from late spring through summer, with the spurs significantly longer than those of most other columbine species. It is an outstanding hummingbird plant, and the extralong nectar spurs are specifically adapted to hummingbird pollination; both hummingbirds and sphinx moths visit the flowers avidly. More tolerant of heat and dry conditions than European columbines, it performs reliably into zone 9 in the West with afternoon shade. Allow to self-sow freely to build a naturalized colony; plants are short-lived perennials (3–5 years) that persist through abundant self-seeding.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall for natural cold-moist stratification; alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 3–4 weeks at 38–40°F and sow in spring. Western columbine self-sows prolifically in cool, moist Pacific Northwest gardens and will naturalize readily if allowed to set seed.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade, mimicking the woodland edge and streamside habitats it occupies naturally along the Pacific Coast from Alaska to Baja California and east through the Rocky Mountains; Aquilegia formosa is distinguished by its vivid red-and-yellow bicolor flowers with short, straight, red spurs — a color combination specifically evolved to attract hummingbirds — and it is one of the most important native hummingbird plants of the Pacific Northwest and mountain West, with hummingbirds and bumblebees being its primary pollinators. It is a natural parent of many popular red-and-yellow hybrid columbines and imparts heat tolerance and reliable performance to its cultivated offspring. Like most columbines, it is a short-lived perennial of 3–5 years that perpetuates itself freely by self-seeding; allow seed to disperse naturally to maintain the planting. Columbine leaf miners may create pale tunnels in the foliage but rarely harm plant health.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall immediately after collection; early meadow rue seed requires cold-moist stratification and declines in viability quickly — fall sowing is strongly preferred over spring. Plants are dioecious (separate male and female individuals), so sow multiple plants to ensure both sexes for seed set.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade under a deciduous canopy, where early meadow rue takes advantage of the winter-spring light window before the forest canopy leafs out; it is one of the earliest eastern North American native wildflowers to bloom, bearing delicate drooping clusters of greenish-purple, petal-less flowers in April and early May that are wind-pollinated and have a quiet, understated beauty quite unlike showier spring ephemerals. The blue-green, finely divided compound leaves — resembling a miniature maidenhair fern in texture and color — are among the most beautiful in the native shade garden and remain ornamental throughout the entire growing season long after bloom. This species is dioecious: male plants bear conspicuous hanging yellow anthers and are showier in flower; female plants are needed for seed set. Note: all parts are mildly toxic if ingested and the plant should not be consumed. Allow to self-sow undisturbed to naturalize across the woodland floor.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed immediately after collection in late summer to early fall — hepatica seed loses viability within weeks of ripening and must not be allowed to dry out before sowing; sow directly into a prepared woodland bed or into pots kept outdoors through winter. Plants are very slow to establish and may take 3–5 years to bloom from seed.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial to full shade under a deciduous canopy, enriched with leaf mold; Hepatica nobilis is a diminutive woodland perennial of extraordinary early-season charm, producing small, jewel-like flowers of blue, lavender, pink, or white — sometimes with many petals in the double forms — from February through early April, typically before the new leaves emerge, making it one of the very first perennials to bloom in late winter and a critical early nectar source for queen bumblebees emerging from overwintering. The distinctive three-lobed, leathery leaves (which give the plant its common name, reflecting their resemblance to a human liver) persist through winter, often bronzing handsomely in cold weather, and are replaced by fresh green foliage after bloom. Hepatica is extremely slow to establish from seed — patience of 3–5 years is required before first bloom — but once settled it is very long-lived and essentially permanent in suitable conditions; do not disturb established plants.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
